records.ety: [OE. beren, AS. beran, beoran, to bear, carry, produce; akin to D. baren to bring forth, G. gebären, Goth. baran to bear or carry, Icel. bera, Sw. bära, Dan. bære, OHG. beran, peran, L. ferre to bear, carry, produce, Gr. fe`rein, OSlav. brati to take, carry, OIr. berim I bear, Skr. bh to bear. √92. Cf. Fertile.]
records.morph: [imp. Bore (bōr) (formerly Bare (br)); p. p. Born (brn), Borne (bōrn); p. pr. & vb. n. Bearing.]

defs: To support or sustain; to hold up.
To support and remove or carry; to convey.
quotes: I 'll bear your logs the while.

defs: To conduct; to bring; -- said of persons.
quotes: Bear them to my house.

defs: To possess and use, as power; to exercise.
quotes: Every man should bear rule in his own house.

defs: To sustain; to have on (written or inscribed, or as a mark), as, the tablet bears this inscription.
To possess or carry, as a mark of authority or distinction; to wear; as, to bear a sword, badge, or name.
To possess mentally; to carry or hold in the mind; to entertain; to harbor
quotes: The ancient grudge I bear him.

defs: To endure; to tolerate; to undergo; to suffer.
quotes: Should such a man, too fond to rule alone, Bear, like the Turk, no brother near the throne.
I cannot bear The murmur of this lake to hear.
My punishment is greater than I can bear.

defs: To gain or win.
quotes: Some think to bear it by speaking a great word.
She was . . . found not guilty, through bearing of friends and bribing of the judge.

defs: To sustain, or be answerable for, as blame, expense, responsibility, etc.
quotes: He shall bear their iniquities.
Somewhat that will bear your charges.

defs: To render or give; to bring forward.
To carry on, or maintain; to have.
To admit or be capable of; that is, to suffer or sustain without violence, injury, or change.
quotes: In all criminal cases the most favorable interpretation should be put on words that they can possibly bear.

defs: To manage, wield, or direct.
To behave; to conduct.
quotes: Hath he borne himself penitently in prison?

defs: To afford; to be to; to supply with.
quotes: His faithful dog shall bear him company.

defs: To bring forth or produce; to yield; as, to bear apples; to bear children; to bear interest.
quotes: Here dwelt the man divine whom Samos bore.
notes: ☞ In the passive form of this verb, the best modern usage restricts the past participle born to the sense of brought forth, while borne is used in the other senses of the word. In the active form, borne alone is used as the past participle.

defs: To produce, as fruit; to be fruitful, in opposition to barrenness.
quotes: This age to blossom, and the next to bear.

defs: To suffer, as in carrying a burden.
quotes: But man is born to bear.

defs: To endure with patience; to be patient.
quotes: I can not, can not bear.

defs: To press; -- with on or upon, or against.
quotes: These men bear hard on the suspected party.

defs: To take effect; to have influence or force; as, to bring matters to bear.
To relate or refer; -- with on or upon; as, how does this bear on the question?
To have a certain meaning, intent, or effect.
quotes: Her sentence bore that she should stand a certain time upon the platform.

defs: To be situated, as to the point of compass, with respect to something else; as, the land bears N. by E.
A bier.

records.ety: [OE. bere, AS. bera; akin to D. beer, OHG. bero, pero, G. bär, Icel. & Sw. björn, and possibly to L. fera wild beast, Gr. fh`r beast, Skr. bhalla bear.]

defs: Any species of the genus Ursus, and of the closely allied genera. Bears are plantigrade Carnivora, but they live largely on fruit and insects.
notes: The European brown bear (Ursus arctos), the white polar bear (Ursus maritimus), the grizzly bear (Ursus horribilis), the American black bear, and its variety the cinnamon bear (Ursus Americanus), the Syrian bear (Ursus Syriacus), and the sloth bear, are among the notable species.

defs: An animal which has some resemblance to a bear in form or habits, but no real affinity; as, the woolly bear; ant bear; water bear; sea bear.
One of two constellations in the northern hemisphere, called respectively the Great Bear and the Lesser Bear, or Ursa Major and Ursa Minor.
Metaphorically: A brutal, coarse, or morose person.
A person who sells stocks or securities for future delivery in expectation of a fall in the market.
notes: ☞ The bears and bulls of the Stock Exchange, whose interest it is, the one to depress, and the other to raise, stocks, are said to be so called in allusion to the bear's habit of pulling down, and the bull's of tossing up.

defs: A portable punching machine.
A block covered with coarse matting; -- used to scour the deck.
To endeavor to depress the price of, or prices in; as, to bear a railroad stock; to bear the market.
